Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections
Division of Nutritional Sciences records
Cornell University. Division of Nutritional Sciences
New York State College of Human Ecology. Department of Human Nutrition and Food
New York State College of Home Economics. Department of Food and Nutrition
29-1-3247
24.1 cubic feet.
203.9 gigabytes.
1947-2018. Correspondence, Printed Materials, Publications, Reports, Photographs, Financial Records, CDs Includes correspondence, reports, grants, course syllabi, meeting minutes, program files, awards, photographs, and department reviews concerning the Division. Collection material in English 5969735
General
Contact Information:
Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu
Compiled by:
Eileen Keating
Date completed:
January 2007
EAD encoding:
RMC Staff, January 2007 Daniel W. Young, February 2010
Date modified:
RMC Staff, August 2018
COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
Includes correspondence, faculty annual reports (some on CDs), grants, course syllabi (on CDs), meeting minutes, program files, awards, photographs, department reviews concerning the Division, and material concerning the Cornell Food and Nutrition Policy Program (CFNPP).
RELATED MATERIAL
See also collections #21-24-3292, Expanded Food and Nutrition Education Program Records; #21-24-1975, Cornell University Cooperative Extension Records; and #23-2-2817, Cornell University College of Human Ecology Records; #29-4-2733, Division of Nutritional Sciences Cooperative Extension Nutrition Program Records for related material.

Additional material and digital versions can be found online here: http://ecommons.cornell.edu/handle/1813/11733
Cite As:
Cornell University Division of Nutritional Sciences records, #29-1-3247. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.
Biographical / Historical
On March 14, 1974 the Board of Trustees voted to abolish the Graduate School of Nutrition and the Department of Human Nutrition and Food in the College of Human Ecology and to establish the Division of Nutritional Sciences, an intercollege unit within the Colleges of Agriculture and Life Sciences and Human Ecology.

Online Trainings and ResourcesCornell NutritionWorks: Online Professional Development2002-2019Crawl of Cornell NutritionWorks website by Archive-It2002-2019
Crawl of Cornell NutritionWorks website by Archive-It [Delivery]
Scope and Contents
This website was created by the Division of Nutritional Sciences to provide online professional development. The Course Catalog listed written and video presentations by experts on a variety of food and nutrition topics. Members could earn personalized certificates of completion for continuing education credit on some topics. Brief articles on current food, nutrition, and food safety topics were also available under Timely Topics. Over its existence, a total of 26,000 people from 190 countries registered as members.
Biographical / Historical
This website was created to replace and expand upon the original Division of Nutritional Sciences Food and Nutrition website. It was funded primarily through grants from USDA.Video presentations from the Cornell NutritionWorks website2002-2019
Preventing childhood obesity, Food and nutrition website videos, Cornell nutrition website (98-2002), UNICEF website coursePreventing Childhood Obesity: Online Training Course2006-2013
Preventing childhood obesity, Food and nutrition website videos, Cornell nutrition website (98-2002), UNICEF website course
Scope and Contents
This online course was designed to train Cooperative Extension and other community nutrition and youth development educators nationally in a new approach to preventing childhood obesity in their communities. The focus was on an ecological approach that involved assessing behavioral, environmental and other factors that contributed to obesity and then determining interventions to change those factors in order to support healthy food and activity behaviors in the community. By the end of the course, participants had created a four-part action plan describing a collaborative approach to preventing childhood obesity customized for their community.
Scope and Contents
The course was offered as a facilitated 6 week course and participants were encouraged to enroll as teams from the same community. In 20 sessions, over 750 people from 50 states and territories and 23 countries enrolled. Of these, 635 people completed the course.
Biographical / Historical
The course was developed and delivered in partnership with eCornell. The course development was funded by a Faculty Innovation in Teaching grant from Cornell and the delivery and evaluation was funded primarily by a grant from USDA's Agriculture and Food Research Initiative (AFRI).Food and Nutrition website1997-2002
Preventing childhood obesity, Food and nutrition website videos, Cornell nutrition website (98-2002), UNICEF website course
Scope and Contents
The website was created to provide up-to-date food and nutrition information online. It included an "Ask the Nutrition Expert" feature which provided a written overview on a topic by a Division of Nutritional Sciences expert and offered the opportunity for people to ask the expert a question. Brief articles on current food, nutrition, and food safety topics were also available under Timely Topics.
Biographical / Historical
Funding for this website was provided primarily through USDA Smith-Lever funds.Programming for Infant and Young Child Feeding: Online Training Course2012-2019
Preventing childhood obesity, Food and nutrition website videos, Cornell nutrition website (98-2002), UNICEF website course
Scope and Contents
This online course was developed to enhance the competencies and build the capacity of UNICEF staff and their counterparts worldwide who are involved in Infant and Young Child Feeding (IYCF) programs in low-and middle-income countries. The content covered IYCF program development, implementation and evaluation. The course was developed in collaboration with UNICEF and was offered for free. More than 14,500 Cornell NutritionWorks members from 181 countries registered for the UNICEF course and more than 4,700 people from 150 countries completed the course.
Biographical / Historical
The course development, delivery, and evaluation were funded by a grant from UNICEF.Crawl of UNICEF site by Archive-It
Scope and Contents
An archived version of the UNICEF course: Programming for Infant and Young Child Feeding website can be found in Archive-It: https://wayback.archive-it.org/2566/20190812123506/https://www.nutritionworks.cornell.edu/UNICEF/home.cfm. The promotional page can be found here:Cornell UNICEF course archived websiteVideo, PDFs and exams from UNICEF course website

Online Trainings and ResourcesCornell NutritionWorks: Online Professional Development2002-2019Crawl of Cornell NutritionWorks website by Archive-It2002-2019
Crawl of Cornell NutritionWorks website by Archive-It [Delivery]
Scope and Contents
This website was created by the Division of Nutritional Sciences to provide online professional development. The Course Catalog listed written and video presentations by experts on a variety of food and nutrition topics. Members could earn personalized certificates of completion for continuing education credit on some topics. Brief articles on current food, nutrition, and food safety topics were also available under Timely Topics. Over its existence, a total of 26,000 people from 190 countries registered as members.
Biographical / Historical
This website was created to replace and expand upon the original Division of Nutritional Sciences Food and Nutrition website. It was funded primarily through grants from USDA.Video presentations from the Cornell NutritionWorks website2002-2019
Preventing childhood obesity, Food and nutrition website videos, Cornell nutrition website (98-2002), UNICEF website coursePreventing Childhood Obesity: Online Training Course2006-2013
Preventing childhood obesity, Food and nutrition website videos, Cornell nutrition website (98-2002), UNICEF website course
Scope and Contents
This online course was designed to train Cooperative Extension and other community nutrition and youth development educators nationally in a new approach to preventing childhood obesity in their communities. The focus was on an ecological approach that involved assessing behavioral, environmental and other factors that contributed to obesity and then determining interventions to change those factors in order to support healthy food and activity behaviors in the community. By the end of the course, participants had created a four-part action plan describing a collaborative approach to preventing childhood obesity customized for their community.
Scope and Contents
The course was offered as a facilitated 6 week course and participants were encouraged to enroll as teams from the same community. In 20 sessions, over 750 people from 50 states and territories and 23 countries enrolled. Of these, 635 people completed the course.
Biographical / Historical
The course was developed and delivered in partnership with eCornell. The course development was funded by a Faculty Innovation in Teaching grant from Cornell and the delivery and evaluation was funded primarily by a grant from USDA's Agriculture and Food Research Initiative (AFRI).Food and Nutrition website1997-2002
Preventing childhood obesity, Food and nutrition website videos, Cornell nutrition website (98-2002), UNICEF website course
Scope and Contents
The website was created to provide up-to-date food and nutrition information online. It included an "Ask the Nutrition Expert" feature which provided a written overview on a topic by a Division of Nutritional Sciences expert and offered the opportunity for people to ask the expert a question. Brief articles on current food, nutrition, and food safety topics were also available under Timely Topics.
Biographical / Historical
Funding for this website was provided primarily through USDA Smith-Lever funds.Programming for Infant and Young Child Feeding: Online Training Course2012-2019
Preventing childhood obesity, Food and nutrition website videos, Cornell nutrition website (98-2002), UNICEF website course
Scope and Contents
This online course was developed to enhance the competencies and build the capacity of UNICEF staff and their counterparts worldwide who are involved in Infant and Young Child Feeding (IYCF) programs in low-and middle-income countries. The content covered IYCF program development, implementation and evaluation. The course was developed in collaboration with UNICEF and was offered for free. More than 14,500 Cornell NutritionWorks members from 181 countries registered for the UNICEF course and more than 4,700 people from 150 countries completed the course.
Biographical / Historical
The course development, delivery, and evaluation were funded by a grant from UNICEF.Crawl of UNICEF site by Archive-It
Scope and Contents
An archived version of the UNICEF course: Programming for Infant and Young Child Feeding website can be found in Archive-It: https://wayback.archive-it.org/2566/20190812123506/https://www.nutritionworks.cornell.edu/UNICEF/home.cfm. The promotional page can be found here:Cornell UNICEF course archived websiteVideo, PDFs and exams from UNICEF course website
